It seemed to be frozen on my screen. You're good, you're live. Okay, then it's just my computer. Yeah, maybe it got zapped too. Sympathy computer problems. Can you hear me, Bob? Yes, I can. Okay, so take it away. Thank you for joining today. I wanted to do a couple of things before we went to the Q&A. The first is just to reinforce how we're vigilant for new research. I'm always scanning the journals, always scanning the psychological and academic outlets for the newest research that might be out. Sometimes I miss something and I will, I'll go back and check in those journals again to see if I had missed something. And I did hit something recently that I had missed. It was, it's something that came out a couple of years ago that solves the following problem. So, suppose you, because of demand for your services or your products, you, you're, you're out. You, maybe if you're a coach, all of the next two months or the next month are blocked out because you've got demand for that and nobody is able to get in and it's not available to them. Or if you're selling products and you've sold out of a particular one, what should you say on your website to define the fact or to describe the fact that that is no longer available, that time or that item. And very often what I see on websites is not currently available or not in stock, something like that, right. A study was done to show that those are wrong headed. If indeed you have this lack of availability because of demand, you need to use a different label. You need to say sold out. And what that does is it engages the rule for social proof. It's not unavailable because you're on vacation or there's a supply chain problem or a manufacturing problem. It's demand. It's the number of people who have sought and and secured that option. That's no longer available. When the researchers looked at that, they found that sold out works dramatically better than either of those other labels, right. And it's because they've added to scarcity something that was already there you've got the principle of scarcity there. But if you can, if you can join it with social proof. Now you get people piling into lists for future purchases to a much greater extent so it's another example of a small big where we just change the words a couple of words. Instead of out of currently unavailable, we call it sold out honestly if it's truly due to demand. All that's all we have to do and it fits exactly with what we're saying we reach in and find something that we truly have. In this case, social proof as the reason for its unavailability, and that social proof then adds on to the existing scarcity principle that's also causing people to want it more. Okay, so now there's a question that sometimes I'm asked how many of these principles can we put into the same message and get these additive effects. The research shows that up to three. You're okay to is I think to is best, but after three of the principles or three appeals. Right. People start feeling that they're being persuaded, rather than informed. Right. And that causes them to pull away from us because of that tendency to avoid being pulled into something. So, I would go with to where they are naturally there. Three would be the most after that. We start getting reverse effects of adding new principles or appeals into the message. So that's one thing I wanted to cover. The other is an as a question I frequently get now that we're promoting the idea of small beings. And that is that. How does this work, actually, what's the mechanism that allows this very popular feature of what we're training. To actually operate the way it does. There's just no question that whenever we present small bigs to audiences, they become very interested in learning about it and it's obviously why that would be the case it's completely understandable. There's a return on investment proposition here that's a very positive one, if all they have to do is change a couple of words or change a sentence or change the sequence of what they have how they make their case. And that's all it's costless. There's no effort involved to speak of that hardly takes any time to do it, and you get these big effects. And that's why it's so important to get a big effect in the network. Because most people believe in something called the rule of proportionality proportionality, the idea that in order to get a big effect, you may you need a big cause, you need something with a lot of force, a lot of energy, a lot of potency in order to get a big effect. And then you know over that that's not the case. Well, how come, if we've seen in many other situations that the rule of proportionality usually works in order to get something big it usually requires that you put a lot of effort or resources into that to that opportunity. Here's the best way I can understand it. We have to think of a small big as a power switch, some something you flick with the littlest effort that energizes a big system of forces. Imagine that you're in a, you're in charge of a football stadiums lighting and for a night game, right. To produce all of the light all of the illumination that comes from those banks of lights around the stadium, you don't have to generate that energy, the energy is already there. You just flip a switch and engage it, you activate it. And then you get the, the consequence. So the big factors, the big sources of power are in the seven principles of influence. All we have to do is ignite them. We have to release that power. We have to just flip a switch a small little action that will do that for us. And the consequence is a big effect. So it turns out the rule for proportionality still is in force. But it's not in the action that we use to activate that big force one of the principles of influence, right, like social proof, right. It's, it's, it's in the small action that we take. The proportionality is still there because the big power that creates the big effect is in the principle. And this is why in our training, we're so focused on not just talking about those principles and the force that they can apply to the, to the persuasion situation. We're also focused on the activators of those principles. That's why we train activators so intensively so that you're, you're, you're informed about what are the steps what are the actions that can activate one or another of these big principles. Those are the things that I was, I wanted to, to address ahead of time before our Q&A. And if you want to ask any questions about either of those, I'm happy to address them. But if you want to ask for larger kinds of approaches that are beyond those two, feel welcome to do that as well. Wonderful Bob that I'm energized now. I was getting a little tired. I might have needed some coffee, but I'm awake now and I'm interested and I'm excited and so is everyone watching. You have to make sure that they know that by demand, not because you're on vacation that month. Right. You have to say those dates are sold out, I'm sorry. That works. That works perfectly and you know now people share their calendars to get it, you know, to allow to schedule meetings. So the ethical parts there if your calendar really is full, they can see it. Exactly. And you just have to point to the social proof reason for that full calendar, rather than these are days that are unavailable for other reasons such as your holidays or something like that. Right. I think we can use many of the same principles, and that is, we can tell people exactly what we have scarcity, for example, the uniqueness of what we offer in terms of the quality or the whatever the features are that we have often it's not a single thing. And compared to our competitors, it's a suite of things that in combination, nobody else has. And if you make that case, you get to use the loss a version of feature. That is, you don't want to lose these things. You don't want to have them and then have them taken away, because you can't get them any anywhere else. Right. The other another principle that works very well is commitment and consistency. Right. Where you ask people, you know, what it is that they would like to enhance about your products or services through co creation surveys that ask them to not. So what are the problems you see with us. No, don't ask that. Ask. If you're asking for feedback, what are the things you would really like us to retain and improve because they're so valuable to you. Right. Then you get that feedback. And don't call it feedback. Thank them for their advice, because the research shows, if you ask people for their feedback or their opinion, you get a critic. You ask them for their advice, and you get a partner. You get a collaborator on the process, which causes them to show greater. positivity towards you and your products and services and and website and also more loyalty to it. You get the rule of unity going there. Question, and I don't know if you've done any research on this, but what are your thoughts around a point of diminishing returns? I would like to just say you're applying more than one persuasion principle, maybe it's five, six, seven, in multiple places. How do you get a feel for too much? Yeah, is too manipulative, maybe, and people sense that, oh, this looks like a, you know, a manipulative situation or desperate situation. You see the landing pages with, you know, 500 testimonials and hey studies and you just feel like, wow, you've really overdone your social proof or your sense of case studies and proof and whatnot, and it becomes exhaustive. What are your thoughts on that? Well, fortunately, there is research on this and we mentioned it a little bit earlier. First of all, if you use two, that's fine provided that they're not in conflict with one another. For example, in the national public radio stations here in the United States, they ask for contributions, they ask for people to give contributions, and they say, please give us a contribution, a charitable contribution. And then they say, and if you do that, we will give you this prize, right, that actually backfired, because you're taking away their sense of altruism and their sense of doing this for a good purpose, and now you're undermining it with money, right? So they have to do things that are aligned with one another. The way we just talked about it with the idea of sold out is completely aligned that, okay, we are, there's a scarcity principle at work when things are not available, right? But there's a reason for why they're not available, which is social proof, and that elevates the impact of the scarcity. So as long as they're aligned, two of them are great. And the answer shows, if you get beyond three separate appeals, separate principles, people start backing away from you, and you actually get a reversal of your impact. You get people bailing out of your offer, because they feel like they're being sold. And that's this quote, people love to buy, but they don't like to be sold. We can't give them the impression that we are operating on them as targets of persuasion. And that happens, even when it's not true, but it happens above three. So, I would say the optimum is two, and you never want to go above three. I would say that those two to three principles apply to a particular page or throughout the, let's say, a website. Throughout the website. Okay. They're going to be, they're going to be cognizant of how they're being sold here. And our great advantage for this material is that the principles of influence typically inform them into a scent. And educate them as to what to do if there's true scarcity in the situation if there's true social proof if there's true authority, all of those things lend themselves to good choices. You, you want to secure things that are rare, waddling in availability, you want to, to get the power of social proof, working in your favor same thing with authority the power of authority they usually counsel you correctly. So, what we have is a set of principles that instruct them in how to behave well for for good outcomes for them. If you add another, they start seeing it as not education, but as a sales device that, you know, that what we're giving them is a series of things designed to push them, rather than inform them into a scent. Yeah, helpful, helpful versus manipulative maybe. Right, right. They just have a different view of what our intent is, if we overload them. Right. In terms of the seven, seven principles but things like baby steps, or confirmation would be different, right, because baby steps in terms of filling out a form would not necessarily be a influence or or persuasion principle. I wouldn't see that as an attempt to push them. Right, right. Just make it easier. Right, so the key is avoid adding things that could be interpreted as device as sales devices, rather than counsel for correct choices. Okay, wonderful. Thank you. I kind of think of it as kind of the same way if you've ever had a someone a suitor or someone trying to date you, and you know one or two calls or an email or a message is nice but then once your bombard they keep on and on and on you back off. That's quite a lot of estimates. So one two or three is plenty of room. Any ideas how you can create. Yes, more of a sense of oh I better do this now. And the research is very clear on this, the shorter the time in which people can it enroll in a program. That's right. So what you can do though, you can offer an enhancement for those who sign up within a month. So it's only available to people who sign up within the next so, so much. After that you can provide a different enhancement for anybody who signs up in the following month or something. I mean, you can, you can arrange for a limited time by what you give in addition to what people typically get in their programs. I think that's possible. Here's another one that a while ago. A set of banks. Arranged for behaviorally trained. What, what's the right word here. Consultants consultants to get into the security systems of all the, the units of the bank, you know, the locations of the banks, right, using no technological defenses, right, against this. They just use behavioral science. They just dressed in a certain way. They carried clipboards. They had connections they said they have a connection with somebody up higher in the organization who arranged for them to come in. And in 90% of the cases, they were able to come in and not just steal data, take away computers, take, take away devices, security devices, right, because people weren't aware of it. They weren't, they weren't aware of the behavioral science tricks that could be employed. You can. I can send you the study, but you can tell people about that study and the critical thing is to tell them about mistakes that were made by somebody else. And that brings another kind of urgency in. I don't, I need to get this done now before somebody hacks me before somebody involved. So you say, you give the example of what happened to others, and who are so regretful that they didn't get your program into place before they were violated. Right. There's a, you know, Charlie Munger, the guy who's Warren Buffett's partner at Berkshire Hath. Right. Whenever he makes a decision, he says he consults his list of inanities, people who made stupid mistakes in their investment choices in the past. He, he reports, he covers them. He writes them down. And then he goes through the inanities that other people will have made so that he won't do it. Right. So, this is important because you're not threatening them by saying you have done something stupid here. You're saying these other people did something stupid so you're not attacking them. Look what happened to these other people. And then they're safe in terms of their, you save their face, they don't have to be seen as stupid. It's these other people who were stupid. And you, you show them how, if these people had done the right thing in the right amount of time, they would have been saved the disaster that befell them. There's research to show that if you give trainees who are firefighters, right. Training that allows them to identify the correct actions that previous fighter fighters have made in various emergency situations and learn from those correct actions. Right. The other half of the fire high fighters get training that's focused on the mistakes that have been made previously by other fighters, firefighters in the past. And then at the end of the training, you evaluate their ability to think strategically and choose the right action to take. It's those who were shown the mistakes of others. The errors of others, you don't attack them. You attack the inanity, the stupidity of people who failed to do it. And you have to say, and then with these kind of testimonials we've talked about, where you can get somebody to say I'm so, I'm so sorry that I didn't engage what in zeal has to offer before we lost this amount of money in an attack. So that's how I would kind of phrase it and that gives you once again, another way to get urgency into the mix. Fantastic. It reminds me Bob of one you when you say you want to be welcoming to people before you go on stage to do your keynotes you what's that you tell you tell about having a smile. Yeah. So one of the things that we've seen is that smiles produce this effect of humanizing and increasing liking between any two parties. And in fact, I was driving in my car. I heard a radio interview with a guy who said he did a study of service organizations where people would come into the location and ask for a realtor or an exercise coach or something like this. And what they said they most wanted when they came in from that person behind the desk was not evidence of competence was not evidence of experience. It was a smile. They wanted a smile first. So when I am on stage. I try to smile broadly at the beginning of my remarks, but I do it in a way that tries to have. I do it twice to have honesty associated with it integrity. A few years ago. I was asked to do a presentation for a charity organization. And they didn't have my budget. Well, it turns out that every year, I do try to find one charity that I can speak to and give a reduced price for them and I said okay I'll do this for you and they said well, you know what, I'll do that for us we will. I'll tell you what we'll do in return on our board of directors. We have somebody who is who, who coaches speakers on how to be more effective. And then you can have that person in the room when you give your keynote for our group. And then afterwards, he'll be willing to donate a morning of his time to go over your talk and your presentation and how to approve it. And so I did that. And then I spent three hours with this gentleman was he was going through my entire presentation almost line for line. And I'll tell you, I don't remember any of it. I don't remember any of it, except one thing that he said, he said, you know, Dr. Chaudhary, you have kind of a big mouth. People would enjoy having you smile broadly at them when you began your presentation, right, it would make a connection with them. And then he said something that I have always remembered. He didn't say, so when you get in front of the audience, smile broadly at them. He said, before you get in front of the audience. Find a reason to smile broadly. And the reason could be something like, well, I'm really well prepared for this I spent a lot of time last night preparing for this, or I know that my material is going to fit very well with the challenges the influence challenges of these individuals or I know that my, I have a friend here in the audience, who's going to be my supporter, and that will make me smile. So whatever it was, it was a genuine smile. Right. So now, before I go on stage, I smile broadly for a reason that produces a true smile. Before the research came out, showing that people recognize phony smiles, they can, they can register when a smile is genuine versus when it's made up when it's just forced. It turns out it has to do with the muscles around the eyes. Anyway, so that has worked very well for me. I, to respond to your point. Eileen, yes, something as humanizing and personalizing as a smile will have enormous consequences. And I have found that as a result. But the, the, the other research that I think I've already presented in one of these groups already is that just the shape of a smile. If you put an upturned line underneath your logo, or in your packaging, or underneath the name of a new model or a new product. People are more likely to choose it because of its association to a smile. That's how primitive it is in us. We want a smile that's the signal of welcoming again. I love that story and I, it was, I remember it, it was just a moment when you first told me that story about the smile and I try and do it myself as well when I go into situations and it just, and it also just makes me happy makes me feel good. Yeah, yeah, because you remind yourself of some reason that you feel good which causes you to have more confidence and more energy before you begin. That's a little small big right there. It doesn't take a lot of time, but it takes a moment before you enter the arena. Yeah. Fantastic. Let's go to Pilar Pilar. How are you. Hi, thank you, Ailee. Thank you very much. I'm going to start with me back again here. Yes. Well, I'm afraid I have a more philosophical question rather than a practical one. I'm quite, well, I've already organized a webinar together with another fellow founding member Patrick who is not here today, and we're going to talk through the commitment principle, consistency and commitment. Well, I've discovered that I don't know whether if it is because we're living in this misinformation, times and fake news and everything. And also because probably consistency is that I would say the long term principle because it takes time to really enhance consistency time after time. So you have suddenly this big question like the new generations, they don't really, they don't really appreciate that much the consistency, but maybe say collateral, but how would you think that understanding the principles of influence can be used to promote more critical thinking and discernment on the misinformation and the fake news and the whole thing. That's a somewhat of a different question than the one you began with. Yeah, well, sorry, it maybe is because it's my collateral, yeah, my thinking and it goes together on the misinformation for one side because I think consistency is not getting that much important. At least in the short term, so I think that people they don't care to say one thing today and another one the next day, and they don't give them at least the importance that we did the old generation. But on top of that, if you have the audience of the new people of the new generations which I teach in the university as you know, Sanline said, okay, how could I just enhance and leverage the principles for both to really demonstrate that consistency is it gives pay out on the long term. And this is good that they stick true to their to their world and and also to promote the critical thinking and discernment for them to learn. Right, so let's let's take those two in order. First of all, as as result as as involves a small big for consistency. There's something you can do, which is to rem simply remind people of what they have already said or done in a public way. For example, research shows that if you remind people of the amount of charity donations that they have given in the past. They become more likely to give to a new charity. Because you don't have to install this desire to be a good person and to share your resources with somebody who needs it, you just remind them that they have done this in the past, which allows them to make a quick choice to go in that direction. It's also the case, for example, that a big problem in hospitals and clinics is doctors. Excuse me, who don't wash their hands between examinations. All you have to do to increase that by 45% is to have a sign over the gel machines or the soap dispensers that says, washing your hands. Significantly significantly reduces infection in your patients. Right. If in. So what why is that a reminder, every healthcare professional as nurse or a doctor has made a commitment to the welfare of their patients. All you have to do is remind them of it. There, they did this study where if you instead of that sign, you put in a sign that says, washing your hands significantly reduces the risk of infection for you. It has no effect. It doesn't remind them of a commitment that they made to their patients. So, simply reminding people as a way to do it with a small big, you don't have to generate the commitment to yourself. Now, the second part about the newer generations, right, who are jumping around. I know why they jump around. It's because they're focused on the new. If you have people below 25 or 30 years old, they're looking for the newest information and so they'll be jumping around to find it. People who are over 50. They are much more susceptible to commitments. If you just remind them of their commitment, they're going to stay congruent with it in fact even increase their commitment to it. So what do you do about that younger group. Give them new information that fits with their commitment. Give them information that they haven't had before that reinforces what they're doing. Give them an example of something that is very aligned with what they had already chosen for you. But make it new and make it probably from coming from a reference of somebody like them. Right, somebody a young person. Right. So I would, I would use those two approaches. But especially for young people. Remind them of the commitment by giving them evidence of something new that wasn't part of the original choice, but, but fits with it. But is a line tied to their and tied to their world with somebody on the same age or something. Okay. Thank you very much. That's very good. And then I'm going to add something. It's a specific study that shows that whenever you are smiling, you are getting a shot of dopamine. So your brain starts thinking that you are in a good position. So it's a retro, it's a feedback. It's a feedback system for you that you're feeling better in the moment that you are. Thank you. Thank you, Pilar. You know, we, I, Lee and I were just talking about how when you smile, you do a yourself a favor because it increases your energy. That's a shot of dopamine and actually you can fake it until you make it, even if it, but that's not for the professional. Right. You get better, you're happier and you get more productive after you self affirm. Right. And I never thought that it might be because you smile. Does this neuroscientist, Dr. Lisa Feldman Barrett, that she has consistently proven that that's the question when your brain knows things or really has the feeling that is in a good place, then all your body gets in a good place. So I used to do these. I think this is very interesting what you're suggesting and I used to do these self affirmations. But what are the things or the things that I'm, I'm grateful for gratitude. I'm sorry, I was saying self affirmations I meant gratitude in the morning and express what you're grateful for. Right. And after a while, I stopped doing it because I kept saying I kept being grateful for the same things. My friends, my, my professional relationships, my, my grandchildren, whatever it was that I would, I would say the same things every time. And it was getting boring. And it wasn't having the same effect. Well, then I attended a lecture by a guy who said, Here's what I do. I break down the gratifications to much more minor things. He was like he said, Wow, I feel so grateful for the pillow. This comfortable pillow that I saw on last night. And for the fact that I'm going to have a breakfast that includes my favorite, my, my favorite scone or whatever, you know, just something small that you can be grateful for. But in each case, it makes you smile. And that's it. It helps yourself with the smile that goes with the dopamine. So thank you very much. Very interesting. Thank you, Pilar.
